questions.question: What are Swarm's two primitives?
options: Graphs and edges
Routines (instructions + tools as a system prompt) and handoffs (a tool that returns another Agent) — the runtime switches active agent on the Agent return value
Queues and workers
Crews and tasks
questions.explanation: OpenAI Swarm (October 2024) distilled orchestration to its minimum: no state machine, no branching DSL. The triage agent's prompt makes it call the right transfer function, and the LLM's tool-calling does the routing.

questions.question: Why did the handoff pattern spread so fast?
options: OpenAI required it for API access
Tiny API surface (two concepts), it reuses production-grade tool calling the model already does, and there is no state-machine or graph DSL to learn — the prompts describe who hands off to whom
It is the only pattern that supports GPT models
It eliminates the need for system prompts
questions.explanation: "Agent = prompt + tools; handoff = function returning agent" fits in a sentence, and Swarm's entire source is a few hundred lines — the cleanest conceptual reference even after the production successor shipped.

questions.question: What is the cost of Swarm's statelessness?
options: Higher latency per call

Memory, continuity, and long-running tasks are the caller's problem — nothing persists between runs, parallelism needs external orchestration, and non-deterministic handoff choices make exact replay hard
It cannot call more than three agents
It requires a GPU per agent
questions.explanation: The framework keeps message history only within a run. These exact gaps are what the OpenAI Agents SDK (March 2025) addressed: built-in session management, guardrails, and tracing — while keeping the handoff primitive.

questions.question: Which task shapes fit the handoff pattern best?
options: Multi-hour research syntheses
Triage (front-line agent routes to specialists), skill-based handoffs, and short bounded conversations like support flows
Massively parallel scraping
Anything requiring full audit replay
questions.explanation: Routing-shaped problems are the sweet spot. Long shared-memory sessions, parallel execution, and audit/replay are where the stateless single-active-agent design struggles.

questions.question: In Swarm, where does the "state machine" actually live?
options: In a YAML config file
Implicitly in the agents' system prompts — each prompt describes when to hand off and to whom; the orchestrator is whichever agent currently holds the conversation
In a central database
In the LLM provider's servers
questions.explanation: There is no explicit graph: "you are a triage agent; if the user asks about refunds, hand off to the refund agent" IS the routing table. That inversion — prompts as topology — is the pattern's defining idea.
